title: If the DMA is fit for purpose why are the gatekeepers winning?
content:
The European Commission says the Digital Markets Act (DMA) was “fit for purpose” and had “positive impact.” But while it certainly has that potential, laissez-faire enforcement and political interference by the Commission undermine its effectiveness and allow gatekeepers to continue to dominate European digital markets.

The post If the DMA is fit for purpose why are the gatekeepers winning? appeared first on European Digital Rights (EDRi).

title: Czech Big Brother Awards 2025: Volkswagen, Meta and the Czech authorities noted as Snoopers of the Year
content:
EDRi member Iuridicum Remedium organised the 21st annual Big Brother Awards in Czech Republic, with Volkswagen, Meta, and the Czech Ministries of the Interior and Industry and Trade highlighted as some of the biggest threats to privacy. Electric cars emerged as a new concern, and data retention and attacks on encryption continued to win big.

The post Czech Big Brother Awards 2025: Volkswagen, Meta and the Czech authorities noted as Snoopers of the Year appeared first on European Digital Rights (EDRi).

title: Greece’s AI Smart Policing system ruled unlawful after €4 million public spending\
content:
A 4 million EUR “Smart Policing” programme enabling the use of AI technologies, including facial recognition software, which was deployed by the Hellenic Police has been ruled unlawful by Greece’s data protection authority. The decision confirmed long-standing concerns raised by EDRi member Homo Digitalis about facial recognition and data protection violations.

The post Greece’s AI Smart Policing system ruled unlawful after €4 million public spending\ appeared first on European Digital Rights (EDRi).

title: EDRi responds to European Commission’s consultation call on the Digital Omnibus
content:
The European Commission opened consultations for the Digital Omnibus, EDRi responded by urging the Commission not to prioritise corporate interests and deregulation over privacy, transparency, and fundamental rights, ultimately putting people at greater risk of data misuse and discrimination.

The post EDRi responds to European Commission’s consultation call on the Digital Omnibus appeared first on European Digital Rights (EDRi).

creator: EDRi

title: Youth organisations demand social media change, not bans
content:
The protection of young people from online harms remains high on the political agenda, but the debate continue to focus on age gates and social media bans. In response, 31 youth organisations and youth activists – the intended recipients of these ‘protective’ measures – have joined forces to speak up against their own exclusion. They warn that the real solution lies in addressing the root cause of the problem: the design and business model of platforms.

The post Youth organisations demand social media change, not bans  appeared first on European Digital Rights (EDRi).

title: The EU AI Office must prioritise setting up the Advisory Forum
content:
35 organisations and researchers call on the EU AI Office to provide clear information on the timeline and process of the establishment of the Advisory Forum, the only formal mechanism ensuring civil society voices are part of AI Act implementation.

The post The EU AI Office must prioritise setting up the Advisory Forum appeared first on European Digital Rights (EDRi).

creator: EDRi

title: Announcing the Summit “Fight for Us, not for Them”: A public interest vision for EU tech policy
content:
Europe’s approach to governance for advancing technology in the digital age is under pressure, with “simplification” proposals hitting the heart of core digital protections. At the same time, civil society is uniquely positioned to co-develop the EU’s vision for public-interest tech laws, policies and practices. Today, civil society organisations announce a strategic convening between European lawmakers, regulators, journalists and key civil society voices for a vision for tech laws and practices that center public interest, data protection and other rights.

The post Announcing the Summit “Fight for Us, not for Them”: A public interest vision for EU tech policy appeared first on European Digital Rights (EDRi).
snippet: Europe’s approach to governance for advancing technology in the digital age is under pressure, with “simplification” proposals hitting the heart of core digital protections. At the same time, civil so
creator: EDRi

title: It’s not just spyware scandals: EU is funding the industry that spies on Europeans
content:
Spyware, an extremely potent technology that turns a personal device into a constant surveillance instrument, was used by the Greek secret services to target dozens of people, including journalists, politicians and business executives. The Greek case marks arguably the first time that the executives of a spyware manufacturer – Intellexa, who developed Predator – will face criminal accountability. Although it did not condemn any Greek authorities for using spyware, this victory is heartening. It also leads to a bigger question: what about the EU’s own complicity in funding the market for spyware, even when we recognise the harms it inflicts?

The post It’s not just spyware scandals: EU is funding the industry that spies on Europeans appeared first on European Digital Rights (EDRi).

title: Europe shouldn’t “move fast and break things” with fundamental rights
content:
The Digital Omnibus proposals, presented as “simplification,” risk weakening essential safeguards in the GDPR, the ePrivacy Directive, and the AI Act. By reducing protections and delaying obligations for high-risk systems, they introduce a logic reminiscent of the tech industry’s “move fast and break things” approach. In digital infrastructures built on large-scale data processing and automated decision-making, however, mistakes do not simply disappear. They become part of the system. This is why regulation is essential to protect people’s rights.

The post Europe shouldn’t “move fast and break things” with fundamental rights appeared first on European Digital Rights (EDRi).
snippet: The Digital Omnibus proposals, presented as “simplification,” risk weakening essential safeguards in the GDPR, the ePrivacy Directive, and the AI Act. By reducing protections and delaying obligations

title: Open Letter: EU lawmakers must safeguard the AI Act
content:
41 organisations and experts are calling on the European Commission, the European Parliament, and the Council to reject the AI Omnibus, and honour their responsibility in upholding and safeguarding the integrity of the AI Act and its implementation without delay.

The post Open Letter: EU lawmakers must safeguard the AI Act appeared first on European Digital Rights (EDRi).

title: The Court of Justice of the European Union condemns France’s police profiling practices
content:
On 19 March 2026, the EU court ruled that France's law allowing law enforcement data collection is disproportionate and in violation of EU rules, as raised by public interest groups like La Quadrature du Net. This is another illegal feature in the French police databases, which must be urgently dismantled.

The post The Court of Justice of the European Union condemns France’s police profiling practices appeared first on European Digital Rights (EDRi).
snippet: On 19 March 2026, the EU court ruled that France's law allowing law enforcement data collection is disproportionate and in violation of EU rules, as raised by public interest groups like La Quadrature
creator: EDRi

title: Safeguarding democratic lawmaking: EDRi’s contribution to Commission consultation on Better Regulations
content:
The European Commission has opened a consultation on its Better Regulation framework. In its response, EDRi raises concerns about the lack of proper impact assessment and the false sense of urgency. Instead of strengthening democratic processes, the current reform risks practices that reduce transparency and limit participation.

The post Safeguarding democratic lawmaking: EDRi’s contribution to Commission consultation on Better Regulations appeared first on European Digital Rights (EDRi).

title: The Digital Omnibus reopens the EU data acquis before it has even been tested
content:
The Digital Omnibus not only targets the GDPR, ePrivacy and AI rules, but also rewrites the EU’s data acquis by merging recent laws into the Data Act. These changes risk weakening safeguards, concentrating power, and creating uncertainty before the framework has even been implemented in practice.

The post The Digital Omnibus reopens the EU data acquis before it has even been tested appeared first on European Digital Rights (EDRi).

creator: EDRi

title: How can the EU protect children online while dismantling the very rules designed to keep them safe?
content:
Protecting children online has become one of the most powerful political narratives in Brussels, yet proposals like the Digital Omnibus risk weakening the very safeguards that make this protection possible. This is a contradiction: can children truly be protected if the rules designed to keep them safe are being dismantled?

The post How can the EU protect children online while dismantling the very rules designed to keep them safe? appeared first on European Digital Rights (EDRi).

title: #PrivacyCamp25: Event summary
content:
On 30 September 2025, policymakers, activists, human rights defenders and academics from Europe and beyond gathered in Brussels and online for Privacy Camp 2025. Together, we explored the theme Resilience and Resistance in Times of Deregulation and Authoritarianism.

The post #PrivacyCamp25: Event summary appeared first on European Digital Rights (EDRi).

title: Europe’s digital laws are not bargaining chips
content:
In reaction to the recent plan to “open a formal dialogue” with the US government on EU tech rules, EDRi and other civil society organisations urge the Commission to halt this plan that risks giving Big Tech a back door to weaken the EU digital rulebook and its enforcement.

The post Europe’s digital laws are not bargaining chips appeared first on European Digital Rights (EDRi).

title: A practical guide to joint investigations: lessons learned from one year of the Civic Journalism Coalition
content:
One year ago, EDRi, European Center for Not-for-Profit Law (ECNL) and Lighthouse Reports launched the Civic Journalism Coalition with the aim of connecting investigative journalists with digital rights and civil society organisations. Today, that partnership is yielding tangible results, from journalistic investigations to a new practical guide for collaboration. Here is an overview of the activities - investigations, workshops and community-building efforts - that have shaped the Coalition.

The post A practical guide to joint investigations: lessons learned from one year of the Civic Journalism Coalition appeared first on European Digital Rights (EDRi).
